---
title: "Chapter 35 — 🛒 Recommender Systems"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 34 · 🌈 Multimodal AI](ch34.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [36 · ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting →](ch36.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Recommender systems are the algorithms that decide what shows up when a store, a streaming service, or a social feed asks "what should we put in front of *this* person *right now*?" They sit in the Applied ML Systems part of the field because they are less a single model than a whole pipeline — data, retrieval, ranking, and feedback — built around one prediction: how much will user $u$ like item $i$? Get it right and you drive most of the engagement on platforms like Netflix, Amazon, YouTube, and Spotify; get it wrong and you bury good items under popular junk.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Applied ML Systems · used to predict and rank items a user will like from sparse interaction data · the one key idea is that *behavior patterns across many users reveal what any one user will want*.
:::

## 35.1 — The recommendation problem and the user–item matrix

Start with the object everything else is built on: the **user–item matrix** $R$. Rows are users, columns are items, and the entry $R_{ui}$ is how user $u$ responded to item $i$ — a star rating, a click, a purchase, or simply blank if they never met. The recommendation task is to fill in the blanks: predict the missing entries, then for each user surface the items with the highest predicted scores.

The defining feature of this matrix is that it is almost entirely empty. A catalog of a million items and ten million users has $10^{13}$ cells, but a typical user has touched a few hundred. This **sparsity** — often well above 99% — is why naive approaches fail and why the whole field exists. You are doing massive extrapolation from a tiny observed fraction.

A second structural fact: there are two ways to fill the blanks, and they define the two classical families. You can lean on *other users' behavior* (collaborative filtering) or on *the items' own attributes* (content-based filtering). Almost every real system blends both.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <text x="10" y="14" fill="currentColor">user–item matrix R (? = to predict)</text>
  <g fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1">
    <rect x="60" y="30" width="240" height="100"/>
    <line x1="120" y1="30" x2="120" y2="130"/><line x1="180" y1="30" x2="180" y2="130"/><line x1="240" y1="30" x2="240" y2="130"/>
    <line x1="60" y1="55" x2="300" y2="55"/><line x1="60" y1="80" x2="300" y2="80"/><line x1="60" y1="105" x2="300" y2="105"/>
  </g>
  <g fill="currentColor" text-anchor="middle">
    <text x="90" y="47">5</text><text x="150" y="47">?</text><text x="210" y="47">3</text><text x="270" y="47">?</text>
    <text x="90" y="72">?</text><text x="150" y="72">4</text><text x="210" y="72">?</text><text x="270" y="72">2</text>
    <text x="90" y="97">4</text><text x="150" y="97">?</text><text x="210" y="97">?</text><text x="270" y="97">5</text>
    <text x="90" y="122">?</text><text x="150" y="122">2</text><text x="210" y="122">4</text><text x="270" y="122">?</text>
  </g>
  <text x="30" y="85" fill="currentColor" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 30 85)">users</text>
  <text x="180" y="146" fill="currentColor" text-anchor="middle">items</text>
</svg>

## 35.2 — Collaborative filtering: neighborhoods

The intuition is the oldest one in recommendation: *people who agreed in the past will agree again*. If you and I both loved the same ten films, and you also loved an eleventh I have not seen, that eleventh is a good bet for me. This is **collaborative filtering (CF)** — it uses only the matrix of interactions, never the content of the items.

The neighborhood (memory-based) version comes in two flavors. **User-based CF** finds users similar to you and recommends what they liked. **Item-based CF** flips it: it finds items similar to ones you already liked, where "similar" means *rated alike by the same people*. Item-based is usually preferred in production because item–item similarities are more stable over time and can be precomputed — Amazon's classic "customers who bought this also bought" is item-based CF.

To make "similar" precise you need a similarity over the rows (or columns) of $R$. **Cosine similarity** measures the angle between two rating vectors; **Pearson correlation** is cosine after subtracting each user's mean, which corrects for the fact that some people rate everything high. For users $a$ and $b$ rating a shared set of items $I$:

$$\text{sim}(a,b) = \frac{\sum_{i \in I} (r_{ai}-\bar r_a)(r_{bi}-\bar r_b)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I}(r_{ai}-\bar r_a)^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I}(r_{bi}-\bar r_b)^2}}$$

A predicted rating is then a similarity-weighted average of what the neighbors gave:

```python
import numpy as np

def predict_user_based(R, u, i, k=2):
    # R: ratings matrix, 0 = missing. Predict R[u,i].
    rated = np.where(R[:, i] > 0)[0]          # users who rated item i
    rated = rated[rated != u]
    mu = R[u][R[u] > 0].mean()                # user u's mean rating
    sims = []
    for v in rated:
        common = (R[u] > 0) & (R[v] > 0)       # items both rated
        if common.sum() == 0: continue
        a, b = R[u][common], R[v][common]
        s = np.dot(a-a.mean(), b-b.mean())
        d = np.linalg.norm(a-a.mean()) * np.linalg.norm(b-b.mean())
        sims.append((s/d if d else 0, v))
    sims = sorted(sims, reverse=True)[:k]      # k nearest neighbors
    num = sum(s * (R[v,i] - R[v][R[v]>0].mean()) for s, v in sims)
    den = sum(abs(s) for s, _ in sims)
    return mu + (num/den if den else 0)        # mean + weighted deviation
```

The pitfall is scale: computing all pairwise similarities is $O(\text{users}^2)$, and the matrix is so sparse that two users often share zero items, making every similarity unreliable. That sparsity problem is exactly what the next idea attacks.

## 35.3 — Matrix factorization and latent factors

Neighborhoods compare raw rating vectors, which breaks when overlap is thin. **Matrix factorization (MF)** sidesteps this by assuming the giant sparse matrix is really the shadow of something small and dense. The idea, made famous by the Netflix Prize, is that both users and items live in the same low-dimensional space of hidden traits called **latent factors** — think "amount of comedy," "amount of darkness," "indie-ness" — never labeled by hand but discovered from the ratings.

Each user $u$ becomes a vector $p_u \in \mathbb{R}^f$ and each item $i$ a vector $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^f$, with $f$ maybe 50–200. The predicted rating is just their dot product — high when the user's tastes align with the item's traits:

$$\hat r_{ui} = p_u^\top q_i = \sum_{k=1}^{f} p_{uk}\, q_{ik}$$

You learn the vectors by minimizing squared error on the *observed* ratings only, with $\ell_2$ **regularization** ($\lambda$) so the factors do not overfit the handful of ratings each user has:

$$\min_{p,q} \sum_{(u,i)\in\mathcal{O}} \left(r_{ui} - p_u^\top q_i\right)^2 + \lambda\left(\lVert p_u\rVert^2 + \lVert q_i\rVert^2\right)$$

Two standard solvers: **stochastic gradient descent** (walk over observed ratings, nudge $p_u$ and $q_i$ against the error) and **alternating least squares** (fix one set, solve the other in closed form, repeat — easy to parallelize). Real systems add **bias terms** $\hat r_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + p_u^\top q_i$ to absorb "this user rates high" and "this item is loved by everyone" before the factors model the interesting interaction.

```python
def sgd_mf(R, f=3, lr=0.01, reg=0.1, epochs=50):
    n, m = R.shape
    P = np.random.normal(0, .1, (n, f))   # user factors
    Q = np.random.normal(0, .1, (m, f))   # item factors
    obs = [(u, i) for u in range(n) for i in range(m) if R[u, i] > 0]
    for _ in range(epochs):
        for u, i in obs:
            e = R[u, i] - P[u] @ Q[i]      # prediction error
            P[u] += lr * (e * Q[i] - reg * P[u])
            Q[i] += lr * (e * P[u] - reg * Q[i])
    return P, Q                            # P @ Q.T fills every blank
```

Why it matters: MF turns an unfillable sparse matrix into two small dense ones, generalizes far better than neighborhoods, and the learned vectors are reusable embeddings. Its limits — it is linear (a plain dot product) and static (one global snapshot) — are exactly what deep recommenders later relax.

::: {.callout-tip}
The dot product means recommendation reduces to **nearest-neighbor search in embedding space**: precompute every item vector, and serving a user is just "find the item vectors closest to $p_u$." This is why vector search (Chapter 32) and recommendation share so much machinery.
:::

## 35.4 — Content-based filtering and hybrids

Collaborative filtering has a blind spot: it knows nothing about an item except who touched it, so a brand-new item with zero interactions is invisible. **Content-based filtering** fixes this by recommending from the item's *own attributes*. Build a feature vector for each item — genre, brand, text keywords (TF-IDF), tags, an image or text embedding — then build a **user profile** as the (weighted) average of the feature vectors of items they liked. Recommend the items whose vectors are most similar to that profile. A reader who liked three hard sci-fi novels gets a fourth because its keyword vector points the same way, even if nobody else has read it yet.

The tradeoff is the mirror image of CF. Content-based handles new items gracefully and gives transparent, explainable reasons ("because you liked X"), but it traps users in a narrow lane — it can only recommend more of what the features already describe, so it rarely surprises you. CF, by contrast, finds non-obvious cross-category hits (people who like this obscure band also love this unrelated podcast) but cannot touch a cold item.

| Aspect | Collaborative filtering | Content-based |
|---|---|---|
| Signal used | other users' interactions | item attributes / features |
| New item (cold) | fails — no interactions yet | works — uses attributes |
| New user (cold) | fails — no history | works once a few prefs known |
| Serendipity | high, cross-domain | low, stays in-lane |
| Explainability | weaker ("similar users") | strong ("similar to X") |

Because their weaknesses are complementary, almost every production system is a **hybrid**. Common recipes: *weighted* (blend the two scores), *switching* (use content-based for cold items, CF once enough data accrues), and *feature combination* (feed both signals into one model — the deep models in the next section do this natively). The hybrid is not a luxury; it is how you survive the cold-start problem, which the next idea names directly.

## 35.5 — Feedback types and the cold-start problem

Two practical realities shape every recommender, and ignoring either one quietly wrecks a model.

The first is the kind of signal you have. **Explicit feedback** is a deliberate rating — five stars, a thumbs-down. It is clean and signed (you learn what users *dislike*) but rare, because most people never rate anything. **Implicit feedback** is behavior logged as a side effect — clicks, watch time, purchases, dwell. It is abundant but tricky: it is **unsigned** (a click is weak positive, but a non-click is *not* a confirmed negative — the user may simply never have seen the item) and confounded by exposure. The standard fix is to treat implicit signals as **confidence-weighted preferences**: a preference $p_{ui}=1$ if any interaction occurred, with a confidence $c_{ui}=1+\alpha\,x_{ui}$ that grows with the count $x_{ui}$, and to learn over *all* cells (the unobserved ones as low-confidence zeros) rather than only the observed ones.

The second reality is **cold start**: the system cannot recommend well to or about an entity it has no data on. It comes in three forms — **new user** (no history to match), **new item** (no interactions, so CF can't place it), and **new system** (no data at all). The escape hatches all inject outside information: content features and item metadata to place cold items, demographics or a quick onboarding survey to seed cold users, and popularity or editorial picks as a sensible default until personal signal accumulates.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Cold-start entity] --> B{What is missing?}
    B -->|New item, no clicks| C[Use content features / metadata]
    B -->|New user, no history| D[Onboarding survey + demographics]
    B -->|Brand-new system| E[Popularity + editorial defaults]
    C --> F[Hand off to CF once interactions accrue]
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Treating every non-interaction as a negative is the classic implicit-feedback mistake. Users can only act on items they were *shown*; an item with no clicks may simply never have been surfaced. Without confidence weighting or proper negative sampling, the model learns the recommender's past exposure, not user preference.
:::

## 35.6 — Deep recommenders and the retrieval/ranking split

Matrix factorization is a single linear dot product. Deep recommenders keep the embedding idea but replace and surround it with neural networks, and — just as important — they organize serving into two stages because you cannot score a million items per request.

That two-stage architecture is the backbone of every large-scale system. **Candidate generation (retrieval)** cheaply narrows millions of items to a few hundred plausible ones; **ranking** then runs an expensive, feature-rich model to order just those few. Retrieval must be fast and is usually approximate; ranking can be slow and precise because it sees a short list.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[millions of items] --> B[Retrieval / candidate generation<br/>cheap, approximate, recall-focused]
    B --> C[hundreds of candidates]
    C --> D[Ranking<br/>rich features, precise, precision-focused]
    D --> E[a few re-ranked results shown]
```

The dominant retrieval design is the **two-tower** model: one neural network (tower) turns the user and context into a vector, a second turns each item into a vector, and relevance is their dot product — exactly MF's geometry, but now the towers can ingest arbitrary features (text, categories, history) through nonlinear layers. Because item vectors don't depend on the user, you precompute them all and serve with approximate nearest-neighbor search (Chapter 32). The key models:

- **Neural collaborative filtering (NCF):** replaces the dot product with an MLP over the concatenated user and item embeddings, letting it learn nonlinear interactions a dot product can't.
- **Two-tower retrieval:** separate user and item encoders trained so matching pairs have high dot product; the workhorse of candidate generation.
- **Wide & Deep:** trains a "wide" linear part (memorizes specific feature crosses, e.g. *installed-app-X-and-viewed-app-Y*) jointly with a "deep" part (generalizes via embeddings) — memorization and generalization in one model.
- **Sequence / session-based:** treats a user's history as an ordered sequence and predicts the next item with an RNN or Transformer (Chapters 17–18), capturing intent that drifts within a session — what you want *now*, not on average.

The reason for the split is economic: ranking models are far too heavy to run on the whole catalog, and retrieval is far too coarse to produce a good final order. Separating them lets each be optimized for what it is good at — recall upstream, precision downstream — which is exactly what the metrics in the next section measure.

## 35.7 — Evaluating recommendations

Recommendation is ranking, not regression, so you evaluate the *ordered list* you show, usually the top $k$. Early MF work reported RMSE on held-out ratings, but a low rating error does not guarantee a good top-10 — what matters is whether the items you put at the top are ones the user actually wanted.

The core **top-$k$ metrics**, intuition first:

- **Precision@k** — of the $k$ items you showed, what fraction were relevant. "How clean is the list?"
- **Recall@k** — of all the items the user would have liked, what fraction made it into your top $k$. "How much did you catch?"
- **MAP (Mean Average Precision)** — averages precision measured at each relevant hit, rewarding relevant items appearing *earlier*, then averages over users.
- **NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain)** — the standard when results have graded relevance and *position* matters. Each hit's gain is discounted by a $\log$ of its rank, then normalized by the best possible ordering, so NDCG $=1$ is a perfect ranking.

$$\text{DCG@k} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{2^{rel_j}-1}{\log_2(j+1)}, \qquad \text{NDCG@k} = \frac{\text{DCG@k}}{\text{IDCG@k}}$$

```python
def ndcg_at_k(ranked_rels, k):
    # ranked_rels: relevance scores in the order shown
    r = np.asarray(ranked_rels[:k], float)
    disc = 1 / np.log2(np.arange(2, len(r) + 2))   # rank discount
    dcg  = ((2**r - 1) * disc).sum()
    ideal = np.sort(r)[::-1]                         # best possible order
    idcg = ((2**ideal - 1) * disc).sum()
    return dcg / idcg if idcg else 0.0
```

Offline metrics on logged data are necessary but never sufficient, because the log only contains items the old system chose to show — so the gold standard remains the **online A/B test** measuring real engagement, retention, or revenue (Chapter 11 covers the evaluation and significance machinery). Beyond accuracy, mature teams also track **coverage** (what fraction of the catalog ever gets recommended) and **diversity** (how varied each list is), because a model that scores well on relevance can still quietly collapse onto a handful of safe items.

::: {.callout-warning}
Offline metrics are biased by the very system that generated the logs: you can only score items users were exposed to, so a new model that surfaces genuinely novel items may look *worse* offline while being better in production. Treat strong offline numbers as a filter, not a verdict.
:::

## 35.8 — Bias, filter bubbles, and feedback loops

Recommenders do not passively observe behavior — they shape it, and that creates failure modes no accuracy metric will catch.

**Popularity bias** is the gravitational pull toward already-popular items. Popular items have the most interactions, so they dominate training data, get recommended more, collect even more interactions, and crowd out the **long tail** of niche items that many users would actually prefer. Left unchecked, the system converges on a bland, blockbuster-only catalog and the diversity that made it useful erodes.

**Filter bubbles** are the per-user version: optimize relentlessly for predicted relevance and you keep showing each user more of what they already engaged with, narrowing their world until the feed is an echo chamber. The cure is to deliberately trade a little accuracy for **diversity, novelty, and serendipity** — re-ranking objectives (such as maximal marginal relevance, or determinantal point processes) that penalize a list for being too internally similar.

The deepest problem is the **feedback loop**. The model's outputs become tomorrow's training inputs: it recommends X, users click X *because it was shown*, the log says "X is good," and the model recommends X even harder. Over time the system trains on its own past decisions and can amplify small initial biases into large, self-reinforcing ones, while genuinely better unshown items never get a chance.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Model recommends] --> B[User interacts<br/>with shown items]
    B --> C[Interactions logged]
    C --> D[Model retrained on log]
    D --> A
    D -.amplifies.-> E[Popularity bias /<br/>narrowing exposure]
```

The standard mitigations all break the loop by reintroducing what the system would otherwise never see: **exploration** (occasionally show uncertain or random items, the bandit idea — sacrifice short-term clicks to learn), **inverse-propensity weighting** to debias logged data by how likely each item was to be shown, and explicit diversity or fairness constraints in the final re-ranking step.

## 35.x — Key takeaways

- The substrate is the **sparse user–item matrix**; the task is to fill its blanks and rank the top items per user.
- **Collaborative filtering** uses interaction patterns (neighborhoods, then **matrix factorization** into latent user/item vectors); **content-based** uses item attributes; real systems are **hybrids** because their weaknesses are complementary.
- **Cold start** (new user / item / system) and the **explicit vs implicit feedback** distinction drive most design choices; never treat a non-click as a confirmed negative without confidence weighting.
- Large-scale serving splits into **retrieval** (cheap, recall-focused, two-tower + ANN search) and **ranking** (rich, precision-focused); deep models — NCF, two-tower, Wide & Deep, sequence/session — extend MF's embedding idea with nonlinearity and context.
- Evaluate the *ranked list* with **precision@k, recall@k, MAP, NDCG**, plus **coverage and diversity**, and confirm with **online A/B tests**; offline metrics are biased by past exposure.
- Recommenders shape behavior: watch for **popularity bias, filter bubbles, and self-reinforcing feedback loops**, and counter them with diversity, exploration, and propensity debiasing.

## 35.y — See also

- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** — the latent-factor / low-rank idea behind matrix factorization.
- **Chapter 32 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search** — approximate nearest-neighbor search, the engine behind two-tower retrieval.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** and **Chapter 17 — Recurrent & Sequence Models** — the backbones of session-based recommenders.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** — ranking metrics and the A/B-testing machinery for online evaluation.
- **Chapter 24 — Model-Free Reinforcement Learning** — bandits and exploration for breaking feedback loops.
- **Chapter 46 — AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance** — filter bubbles, bias amplification, and fairness in deployed recommenders.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 34 · 🌈 Multimodal AI](ch34.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [36 · ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting →](ch36.html)
